api/index.html.title: API Reference | Ashes of Velsingrad Documentation
api/index.html.summary: API Reference Welcome to the Ashes of Velsingrad API documentation. This section contains the automatically generated API reference for all public classes, interfaces, and methods in the codebase. Overview This API documentation is automatically generated from XML documentation comments in the C# source code. It provides detailed information about all public APIs, including classes, methods, properties, events, and their usage examples. Quick Navigation 🎮 Core Game Systems Namespace Description Key Classes AshesOfVelsingrad.Actors Game entities and characters BaseActor, Player, Enemy AshesOfVelsingrad.Components Reusable game components HealthComponent, MovementComponent AshesOfVelsingrad.Systems Game logic systems CombatSystem, InventorySystem AshesOfVelsingrad.Managers Global managers GameManager, AudioManager 🖥️ User Interface Namespace Description Key Classes AshesOfVelsingrad.UI.Menus Game menus MainMenu, PauseMenu, SettingsMenu AshesOfVelsingrad.UI.HUD In-game interface HealthBar, MiniMap, InventoryPanel AshesOfVelsingrad.UI.Dialogs Modal dialogs DialogBox, ConfirmationDialog 📊 Data & Utilities Namespace Description Key Classes AshesOfVelsingrad.Data Data structures ItemData, CharacterData, GameSettings AshesOfVelsingrad.Utilities Helper classes MathHelpers, StringExtensions Architecture Overview The API is organized following our component-based architecture: graph TB subgraph "Core Layer" BaseActor[BaseActor] BaseComponent[BaseComponent] BaseManager[BaseManager] end subgraph "Game Systems" GameManager[GameManager] CombatSystem[CombatSystem] InventorySystem[InventorySystem] end subgraph "Components" HealthComponent[HealthComponent] MovementComponent[MovementComponent] InteractionComponent[InteractionComponent] end subgraph "Actors" Player[Player] Enemy[Enemy] NPC[NPC] end BaseActor --> Player BaseActor --> Enemy BaseActor --> NPC BaseComponent --> HealthComponent BaseComponent --> MovementComponent BaseComponent --> InteractionComponent BaseManager --> GameManager Player --> HealthComponent Player --> MovementComponent Key Design Patterns Component Pattern Most game entities inherit from BaseActor and use components for modular functionality: // Example usage (see full documentation in BaseActor) var player = new Player(); player.AddComponent(); player.AddComponent(); Manager Pattern Global systems follow the singleton manager pattern: // Example usage (see full documentation in GameManager) GameManager.Instance.ChangeState(GameState.Playing); Event-Driven Communication Systems communicate through Godot signals: // Example usage (see full documentation in HealthComponent) healthComponent.HealthChanged += OnHealthChanged; healthComponent.Death += OnPlayerDeath; Common Usage Patterns Creating Game Entities // Standard actor creation pattern var enemy = new Enemy(); enemy.AddComponent(new HealthComponent { MaxHealth = 50 }); enemy.AddComponent(new MovementComponent { Speed = 200.0f }); // See: BaseActor.AddComponent() Handling Events // Standard event subscription pattern public override void _Ready() { var combat = GetNode("CombatSystem"); combat.DamageDealt += OnDamageDealt; } // See: Signal attribute documentation Managing Game State // Standard state management pattern GameManager.Instance.ChangeState(GameState.MainMenu); SceneManager.Instance.ChangeScene("MainMenu"); // See: BaseManager pattern documentation API Conventions Naming Conventions Following our coding standards: Classes: PascalCase (PlayerController, InventorySystem) Methods: PascalCase (GetComponent, TakeDamage) Properties: PascalCase (CurrentHealth, MaxSpeed) Events/Signals: PascalCase (HealthChanged, PlayerDied) Private Fields: camelCase with underscore (_currentHealth, _isAlive) Signal Naming All Godot signals follow the EventHandler convention: [Signal] public delegate void HealthChangedEventHandler(int currentHealth, int maxHealth); [Signal] public delegate void DeathEventHandler(); Export Attributes Designer-configurable values use the [Export] attribute: [Export] public float Speed { get; set; } = 300.0f; [Export] public int MaxHealth { get; set; } = 100; Documentation Standards XML Documentation All public APIs include XML documentation: /// /// Applies damage to the character and handles death logic. /// /// Amount of damage to apply (must be positive) /// Type of damage for resistance calculations /// True if the character died from this damage public bool TakeDamage(int damage, DamageType damageType) Code Examples Most class documentation includes usage examples: /// /// /// var health = new HealthComponent { MaxHealth = 100 }; /// health.TakeDamage(25); /// Console.WriteLine($"Health: {health.CurrentHealth}/100"); /// /// Integration with Game Engine Godot Node Integration All main classes inherit from appropriate Godot nodes: Actors: Inherit from CharacterBody2D or RigidBody2D Components: Inherit from Node Managers: Inherit from Node (AutoLoad singletons) UI Elements: Inherit from Control or CanvasLayer Scene Integration Classes are designed to work with Godot's scene system: // Typical scene setup public override void _Ready() { // Initialize components // Connect signals // Set up references } Testing Integration The API is designed for testability with GdUnit4: [TestSuite] public class HealthComponentTests { [TestCase] public void Should_ReduceHealth_When_TakingDamage() { // See: Testing documentation for full examples } } For testing examples, see our Testing Guide. Performance Considerations Memory Management Use object pooling for frequently instantiated objects Dispose of resources properly in _ExitTree() Cache node references instead of using GetNode() repeatedly Update Optimization Use _PhysicsProcess() for physics-related updates Use _Process() for frame-rate dependent updates Batch operations where possible For detailed performance guidelines, see our Architecture Documentation. Migration & Versioning API Stability Public APIs: Stable, changes will be documented in release notes Internal APIs: May change without notice Experimental APIs: Marked with [Obsolete] or XML comments Breaking Changes Breaking changes are documented in: Release notes Migration guides [Obsolete] attributes with migration instructions Getting Help Documentation Issues If you find missing or incorrect documentation: Check the source code for updated XML comments Create an issue on GitHub Ask on our Discord Usage Examples For more comprehensive examples: Getting Started Guide Architecture Documentation Testing Guide Source Code Examples Contributing To contribute to the API or its documentation: Read our Contributing Guide Follow our Coding Standards Include comprehensive XML documentation Add unit tests for new functionality Last Updated: Auto-generated on build Version: Current development branch Engine: Godot 4.4.1 (Mono/.NET 9.0)

docs/contributing.html.title: CONTRIBUTING.md | Ashes of Velsingrad Documentation
docs/contributing.html.summary: CONTRIBUTING.md Language / Langue: 🇬🇧 English | 🇫🇷 Français 🇬🇧 Contributing Guide Thank you for considering contributing to Ashes of Velsingrad! We are a small indie team, and we want to keep the code clean, maintainable, and consistent with our design vision. Please read these guidelines carefully before you start working. 💡 Branching model We use three main branches: develop: main working branch. All features and fixes start here. canary: beta branch. We merge develop into canary when we want to test features in a pre-release context. release: final release-ready branch. We only merge into release when canary is fully tested and stable. Never push directly to canary or release! 🚀 How to contribute 1️⃣ Fork and clone the repo git clone <your fork url> cd ashes-of-velsingrad 2️⃣ Create a feature branch from develop git checkout develop git checkout -b feat/your-feature-name 3️⃣ Follow code standards Use English for code comments and identifiers. Keep commits small and meaningful (avoid huge "dump everything" commits). Include tests or test cases whenever possible. Document new functions or modules clearly. 4️⃣ Commit message convention We follow the Conventional Commits convention to maintain a clear and consistent history. Mandatory format <type>(<scope>): <description> [optional body] [optional footer] Allowed types Type Description Example feat New feature feat(combat): add critical hit system fix Bug fix fix(inventory): prevent null item crash docs Documentation only docs(readme): update build instructions style Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. style(player): fix code formatting refactor Code refactoring without functionality change refactor(combat): simplify damage calculation test Adding or modifying tests test(inventory): add unit tests for sorting chore Maintenance, config, tools chore(ci): update GitHub Actions workflow perf Performance improvements perf(rendering): optimize shadow calculations Available scopes Scope Description Examples player Player system (movement, stats, etc.) feat(player): add dodge mechanic combat Combat system fix(combat): balance damage formula inventory Inventory/item management feat(inventory): add item sorting ui User interface fix(ui): menu button alignment audio Sound and music feat(audio): add ambient sounds level Level design, environment feat(level): add destructible objects ai Artificial intelligence/enemies fix(ai): improve pathfinding save Save system fix(save): prevent data corruption network Multiplayer (if applicable) feat(network): add lobby system build Build/deployment system chore(build): optimize export settings config Project configuration chore(config): update project settings Description rules Length: Maximum 50 characters Language: English only Style: Imperative present ("add" not "adds" or "added") Case: First letter lowercase Punctuation: No ending period Valid examples feat(player): add wall jump ability fix(combat): resolve enemy freeze on death docs(api): document new weapon system refactor(inventory): extract item validation logic test(save): add integration tests for game state chore(deps): update Godot to 4.4.1 perf(rendering): cache frequently used shaders Automatic validation To avoid errors, we recommend using VSCode extensions and Git hooks: Method 1: VSCode Extensions (Recommended) Install these VSCode extensions: Conventional Commits (by vivaxy) - Strongly recommended Git Commit Plugin (by redjue) - Optional, adds commit templates GitLens (by GitKraken) - Optional, better Git integration Configure the Conventional Commits extension in VSCode settings: // .vscode/settings.json { "conventionalCommits.scopes": [ "player", "combat", "inventory", "ui", "audio", "level", "ai", "save", "network", "build", "config" ], "conventionalCommits.showEditor": false, "conventionalCommits.promptBody": false, "conventionalCommits.promptFooter": false } Method 2: Git Hooks Create a simple commit validation script: # .git/hooks/commit-msg (make it executable: chmod +x .git/hooks/commit-msg) #!/bin/bash commit_message=$(cat "$1") # Check if commit message follows conventional format if ! echo "$commit_message" | grep -qE "^(feat|fix|docs|style|refactor|test|chore|perf)(\(.+\))?: .+"; then echo "❌ Invalid commit message format!" echo "Format: <type>(<scope>): <description>" echo "Example: feat(player): add wall jump ability" exit 1 fi # Check if scope is valid (optional) scope=$(echo "$commit_message" | grep -oE "\(.+\)" | sed 's/[()]//g') if [ -n "$scope" ]; then valid_scopes="player|combat|inventory|ui|audio|level|ai|save|network|build|config" if ! echo "$scope" | grep -qE "^($valid_scopes)$"; then echo "⚠️ Warning: '$scope' is not a standard scope" echo "Valid scopes: player, combat, inventory, ui, audio, level, ai, save, network, build, config" fi fi echo "✅ Commit message format is valid" Method 3: Global Git Template Create a commit template to guide team members: # .gitmessage # <type>(<scope>): <subject> # # <body> # # <footer> # Types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore, perf # Scopes: player, combat, inventory, ui, audio, level, ai, save, network, build, config # Subject: imperative, present tense, lowercase, no period # Body: explain what and why (optional) # Footer: breaking changes, issues closed (optional) Then configure Git to use it: git config commit.template .gitmessage In case of error If your commit is rejected: Check the type and scope used Follow the exact format: type(scope): description Avoid custom types like "feature", "add", "update" Special commits Breaking changes feat(combat)!: redesign damage system BREAKING CHANGE: old damage calculations are no longer compatible Multiple scopes feat(player,combat): add stamina system affecting attacks Without scope (discouraged) chore: update .gitignore 5️⃣ Submit a pull request (PR) Target develop. Add a clear PR description: what, why, screenshots or logs if relevant. Reference issues (e.g., Fixes #42). 6️⃣ Code review Another team member reviews your PR. Address feedback before merging. Once approved, it will be merged into develop. 🧹 Other practices Use conventional commit messages (see above). Check your code with linters and static analysis tools (we provide configs). Update relevant documentation if needed. 🏗️ Architectural Decisions When making significant architectural changes: Review the Architecture Documentation Discuss with the team on Discord before major refactoring Update relevant documentation Consider backward compatibility 💬 Questions? If something is unclear, ask in the team Discord or open a discussion in the repository. 🇫🇷 Guide de contribution Merci d’envisager de contribuer à Ashes of Velsingrad ! Nous sommes une petite équipe indépendante, et nous tenons à garder un code propre, maintenable et cohérent avec notre vision artistique. Merci de lire ces instructions attentivement avant de commencer. 💡 Modèle de branches Nous utilisons trois branches principales : develop : branche principale de travail. Toutes les features et correctifs commencent ici. canary : branche bêta. On merge develop dans canary quand on veut tester les fonctionnalités en contexte pré-release. release : branche finale prête à publier. On merge seulement canary dans release quand tout est stable et validé. Ne jamais pousser directement sur canary ou release ! 🚀 Comment contribuer 1️⃣ Forker et cloner le dépôt git clone <url de votre fork> cd ashes-of-velsingrad 2️⃣ Créer une branche feature à partir de develop git checkout develop git checkout -b feat/nom-de-votre-feature 3️⃣ Respecter les standards Utiliser l’anglais pour les commentaires et identifiants. Commits petits et explicites (éviter les gros commits "dump"). Ajouter des tests si possible. Bien documenter les nouvelles fonctions ou modules. 4️⃣ Commit message convention Nous suivons la convention Conventional Commits pour maintenir un historique clair et cohérent. Format obligatoire <type>(<scope>): <description> [body optionnel] [footer optionnel] Types autorisés Type Description Exemple feat Nouvelle fonctionnalité feat(combat): add critical hit system fix Correction de bug fix(inventory): prevent null item crash docs Documentation uniquement docs(readme): update build instructions style Formatage, point-virgules manquants, etc. style(player): fix code formatting refactor Refactoring sans changement de fonctionnalité refactor(combat): simplify damage calculation test Ajout ou modification de tests test(inventory): add unit tests for sorting chore Maintenance, config, outils chore(ci): update GitHub Actions workflow perf Amélioration des performances perf(rendering): optimize shadow calculations Scopes disponibles Scope Description Exemples player Système du joueur (mouvement, stats, etc.) feat(player): add dodge mechanic combat Système de combat fix(combat): balance damage formula inventory Gestion inventaire/objets feat(inventory): add item sorting ui Interface utilisateur fix(ui): menu button alignment audio Sons et musique feat(audio): add ambient sounds level Design de niveau, environnement feat(level): add destructible objects ai Intelligence artificielle/ennemis fix(ai): improve pathfinding save Système de sauvegarde fix(save): prevent data corruption network Multijoueur (si applicable) feat(network): add lobby system build Système de build/déploiement chore(build): optimize export settings config Configuration projet chore(config): update project settings Règles de description Longueur : Maximum 50 caractères Langue : Anglais uniquement Style : Impératif présent ("add" pas "adds" ou "added") Majuscule : Première lettre en minuscule Ponctuation : Pas de point final Exemples valides feat(player): add wall jump ability fix(combat): resolve enemy freeze on death docs(api): document new weapon system refactor(inventory): extract item validation logic test(save): add integration tests for game state chore(deps): update Godot to 4.2.1 perf(rendering): cache frequently used shaders Validation automatique Pour éviter les erreurs, nous recommandons d'utiliser les extensions VSCode et les Git hooks : Méthode 1 : Extensions VSCode (Recommandée) Installez ces extensions VSCode : Conventional Commits (par vivaxy) - Recommandée fortement Git Commit Plugin (par redjue) - Optionnel, ajoute des templates de commit GitLens (par GitKraken) - Optionnel, meilleure intégration Git Configurez l'extension Conventional Commits dans les paramètres VSCode : // .vscode/settings.json { "conventionalCommits.scopes": [ "player", "combat", "inventory", "ui", "audio", "level", "ai", "save", "network", "build", "config" ], "conventionalCommits.showEditor": false, "conventionalCommits.promptBody": false, "conventionalCommits.promptFooter": false } Méthode 2 : Git Hooks Créez un script de validation simple : # .git/hooks/commit-msg (le rendre exécutable : chmod +x .git/hooks/commit-msg) #!/bin/bash commit_message=$(cat "$1") # Vérifier si le message suit le format conventionnel if ! echo "$commit_message" | grep -qE "^(feat|fix|docs|style|refactor|test|chore|perf)(\(.+\))?: .+"; then echo "❌ Format de message de commit invalide !" echo "Format : <type>(<scope>): <description>" echo "Exemple : feat(player): add wall jump ability" exit 1 fi # Vérifier si le scope est valide (optionnel) scope=$(echo "$commit_message" | grep -oE "\(.+\)" | sed 's/[()]//g') if [ -n "$scope" ]; then valid_scopes="player|combat|inventory|ui|audio|level|ai|save|network|build|config" if ! echo "$scope" | grep -qE "^($valid_scopes)$"; then echo "⚠️ Attention : '$scope' n'est pas un scope standard" echo "Scopes valides : player, combat, inventory, ui, audio, level, ai, save, network, build, config" fi fi echo "✅ Format du message de commit valide" Méthode 3 : Template Git global Créez un template de commit pour guider les membres de l'équipe : # .gitmessage # <type>(<scope>): <subject> # # <body> # # <footer> # Types : feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore, perf # Scopes : player, combat, inventory, ui, audio, level, ai, save, network, build, config # Subject : impératif, présent, minuscule, pas de point # Body : expliquer quoi et pourquoi (optionnel) # Footer : breaking changes, issues fermées (optionnel) Puis configurez Git pour l'utiliser : git config commit.template .gitmessage En cas d'erreur Si votre commit est rejeté : Vérifiez le type et le scope utilisés Respectez le format exact : type(scope): description Évitez les types personnalisés comme "feature", "add", "update" Commits spéciaux Breaking changes feat(combat)!: redesign damage system BREAKING CHANGE: old damage calculations are no longer compatible Multiples scopes feat(player,combat): add stamina system affecting attacks Sans scope (découragé) chore: update .gitignore 5️⃣ Soumettre un pull request (PR) Cibler develop. Ajouter une description claire : quoi, pourquoi, captures d’écran ou logs si nécessaire. Référencer les issues (ex: Fixes #42). 6️⃣ Review Un membre de l’équipe relit votre PR. Apporter les corrections demandées avant merge. Une fois approuvée, la PR est mergée dans develop. 🧹 Autres bonnes pratiques Utiliser des messages de commit conventionnels (voir ci-dessus). Vérifier le code avec les linters et outils d’analyse statique (configs fournies). Mettre à jour la documentation concernée. 🏗️ Décisions architecturales Lors de modifications architecturales importantes : Consultez la documentation d’architecture Discutez avec l’équipe sur Discord avant tout refactoring majeur Mettez à jour la documentation concernée Prenez en compte la rétrocompatibilité 💬 Des questions? Si quelque chose n'est pas clair, merci de demander sur le Discord de l'équipe ou d'ouvrir une discussion dans le dépôt.

docs/game-design/combat-system.html.title: ⚔️ Combat System | Ashes of Velsingrad Documentation
docs/game-design/combat-system.html.summary: ⚔️ Combat System The combat system in Ashes of Velsingrad is tactical and strategic, emphasizing positioning, terrain usage, and careful resource management. Core Mechanics Turn-Based Tactical Combat Combat occurs on a grid-based battlefield where positioning is crucial: Initiative System: Determines turn order based on speed and tactical awareness Action Points: Each unit has limited actions per turn (move, attack, cast spell, use item) Opportunity Attacks: Moving past enemies triggers counterattacks Flanking: Attacking from sides or behind provides damage bonuses Terrain Effects The battlefield is more than just decoration: Terrain Type Effect Strategic Use High Ground +15% accuracy, +10% damage Archer positioning Cover +25% dodge chance Protection from ranged Mud/Water -50% movement speed Area denial Fire 5 damage per turn Damage over time zones Ice Slip chance on movement Movement control Weather System Environmental conditions affect combat: Rain: +20% lightning damage, -10% fire damage Fog: -30% ranged accuracy, +stealth effectiveness Snow: -1 movement per turn, fire spells melt snow Sandstorm: -50% visibility, earth spells more effective Unit Types and Roles The Iron Crows Each member of your mercenary company has distinct roles: Kaelen Voss (Commander) Role: Versatile leader with dark magic Abilities: Command skills, curse magic, weapon mastery Special: Corruption system affects available abilities Fighters Role: Front-line combat specialists Abilities: High HP, armor, weapon skills Specializations: Guardian (tank), Berserker (damage), Duelist (mobility) Rangers Role: Ranged combat and reconnaissance Abilities: Bow mastery, tracking, survival skills Specializations: Marksman (damage), Scout (mobility), Hunter (tracking) Mages Role: Elemental magic and battlefield control Abilities: Spell casting, elemental mastery, arcane knowledge Specializations: Elementalist (damage), Controller (battlefield), Healer (support) Rogues Role: Stealth, sabotage, and precision strikes Abilities: Stealth, lockpicking, backstab, poison Specializations: Assassin (damage), Infiltrator (stealth), Saboteur (utility) Combat Mechanics Action Economy Each unit's turn consists of: Movement Phase: Move up to movement range Action Phase: Choose one primary action: Attack: Weapon or spell attack Full Defense: +50% dodge until next turn Charge: Move extra distance with attack bonus Prepare: Ready an action for enemy movement Free Actions: Unlimited (speak, drop item, activate passive abilities) Damage and Health Health System Hit Points (HP): Current health Stamina: Used for special abilities Morale: Affects accuracy and damage Damage Types Physical: Reduced by armor Elemental: Affected by resistances Curse: Ignores most defenses True: Cannot be reduced Status Effects Bleeding: Damage over time Burning: Fire damage each turn Frozen: Cannot act for one turn Cursed: Various negative effects Blessed: Various positive effects Magic in Combat Magic is powerful but comes with risks: Spell Schools Fire: High damage, area effects, burning Water: Healing, freezing, battlefield control Earth: Defense, terrain manipulation, crushing Air: Lightning, speed, battlefield mobility Light: Healing, buffs, curse removal Darkness: Debuffs, lifesteal, fear effects Corruption System Using dark magic increases Kaelen's corruption: 0-25%: No penalties 26-50%: -10% light magic effectiveness 51-75%: Random dark magic surges 76-100%: Risk of losing control Advanced Tactics Formation Combat Positioning your units effectively: Phalanx: Shields front, spears behind Archer Screen: Ranged behind melee protection Pincer: Flank enemies from multiple sides Retreat Formation: Organized withdrawal Environmental Interaction Destructible Terrain: Break walls, collapse ceilings Interactive Objects: Push rocks, activate switches Elevation Changes: Use stairs, ramps, cliffs Spell Combinations: Freeze water, ignite oil, electrify metal Morale and Leadership Morale Effects High Morale: +10% accuracy, +5% damage Broken Morale: -20% accuracy, flee chance Inspiring Presence: Kaelen's leadership affects nearby allies Leadership Abilities Rally: Restore morale to nearby units Tactical Strike: Coordinate multi-unit attacks Retreat: Organized withdrawal with minimal losses Last Stand: Boost damage when outnumbered Enemy AI AI Behavior Types Aggressive: Rush forward, prioritize damage Defensive: Hold position, protect objectives Tactical: Use terrain, focus fire, retreat when wounded Berserker: Ignore self-preservation, maximum aggression Support: Stay back, buff allies, heal wounded Faction Combat Styles Imperial Soldiers Tactics: Formation fighting, discipline Strengths: Armor, coordination, ranged support Weaknesses: Inflexible, vulnerable to magic Inquisition Forces Tactics: Anti-magic focus, holy weapons Strengths: Curse resistance, healing, undead immunity Weaknesses: Vulnerable to overwhelming force Corrupted Enemies Tactics: Unpredictable, fear effects, regeneration Strengths: High damage, status effects, numbers Weaknesses: Light magic, low organization Wild Creatures Tactics: Pack hunting, environmental advantages Strengths: Speed, natural weapons, terrain knowledge Weaknesses: Low intelligence, predictable patterns Tips for Success General Strategy Control the high ground whenever possible Use terrain to your advantage Focus fire on priority targets Protect your wounded - retreat is sometimes necessary Manage resources - don't waste mana or stamina Combat Preparation Scout ahead when possible Choose appropriate equipment for expected enemies Plan spell usage to minimize corruption Position units according to their strengths Emergency Tactics Fighting retreat when overwhelmed Last stand formations when surrounded Sacrifice plays to save the majority Environmental escapes using spells or terrain Master these systems to lead the Iron Crows to victory against impossible odds!

docs/game-design/core-gameplay.html.title: Core Gameplay Loop | Ashes of Velsingrad Documentation
docs/game-design/core-gameplay.html.summary: Core Gameplay Loop The core gameplay of Ashes of Velsingrad is built around a cycle of exploration, tactical combat, and meaningful choice-making that shapes both Kaelen's character and the world around him. 🔄 The Main Loop 1. 🌲 Exploration & Survival Players traverse a war-torn world filled with dangers and opportunities: World Navigation: Overworld Map: Navigate between regions, each with unique challenges and stories Local Exploration: Detailed exploration of villages, ruins, and wilderness areas Environmental Storytelling: Discover the world's history through visual cues and hidden details Resource Management: Food & Supplies: Keep your company fed and equipped Medicine: Treat injuries and illnesses that can affect combat performance Equipment: Maintain and upgrade weapons, armor, and magical items Morale: Manage the psychological well-being of your companions Discovery Elements: Hidden Locations: Secret areas with valuable loot or important story elements Ancient Ruins: Explore pre-Fall civilizations and uncover lost knowledge Survivor Encounters: Meet NPCs with their own stories, needs, and potential as allies 2. 📜 The Challenge Board System Central to the game's structure is the Challenge Board, found in taverns, refugee camps, and safe zones: Contract Types: Type Description Rewards Risks Assassination Eliminate specific targets High coin, reputation with some factions Moral corruption, enemies Rescue Save captured or trapped individuals Reputation, potential allies Time pressure, dangerous locations Sabotage Disrupt enemy operations Strategic advantages, resources Stealth challenges, faction hostility Investigation Uncover secrets and solve mysteries Information, unique items Complex puzzles, moral dilemmas Escort Protect individuals during travel Steady income, connections Combat encounters, responsibility Bounty Hunting Track down criminals and fugitives Variable rewards based on difficulty Dangerous opponents, moral ambiguity Dynamic Consequences: Completed contracts affect faction standings Failed missions can have lasting story implications Some contracts become unavailable based on your moral alignment Success builds reputation, opening new opportunities 3. ⚔️ Tactical Combat Combat in Ashes of Velsingrad emphasizes strategy over reflexes: Turn-Based Strategy: Initiative System: Speed and circumstances determine turn order Action Points: Each character has limited actions per turn Positioning: Terrain, elevation, and formation are crucial Environmental Hazards: Use the battlefield to your advantage Combat Depth: Combo Attacks: Chain abilities between party members Elemental Interactions: Fire spreads, water conducts electricity, earth blocks paths Status Effects: Poison, burning, fear, and corruption affect long-term strategy Equipment Impact: Different weapons and armor significantly change tactics Stakes and Consequences: Permanent Injury: Characters can suffer lasting wounds Death and Resurrection: Fallen allies can be brought back through dark magic—but at what cost? Reputation Impact: How you fight affects how the world sees you 4. 🏕️ Company Management Your band of survivors is more than just a combat party: Companion Dynamics: Individual Personalities: Each character has unique traits, fears, and motivations Loyalty System: Actions and decisions affect how companions view Kaelen Personal Quests: Deep storylines for each major companion Conflict Resolution: Mediate disputes and manage group dynamics Camp Management: Base Building: Improve your mobile camp with better equipment and facilities Crafting System: Create and upgrade equipment using gathered resources Training: Develop character abilities and learn new skills Rest and Recovery: Heal wounds, restore magic, and boost morale Recruitment: Iron Crows Reunion: Search for scattered members of your former company New Allies: Recruit survivors, deserters, and others seeking purpose Loyalty Tests: Prove worthy of their trust through actions, not words 5. 🔥 Power Progression & Moral Alignment Character development in Ashes of Velsingrad is tied directly to moral choices: Alignment Paths: ⚔️ Vengeance Path: Abilities: Enhanced damage, intimidation, fear-based magic Playstyle: Aggressive tactics, ruthless efficiency World Impact: NPCs fear but respect your power Corruption Risk: High - anger feeds the curse within 🛡️ Redemption Path: Abilities: Healing magic, protective spells, inspiration Playstyle: Defensive strategies, minimal casualties World Impact: NPCs trust and seek your protection Corruption Risk: Low - but harder to gain raw power 👑 Domination Path: Abilities: Dark magic, mind control, necromancy Playstyle: Overwhelming force, fear tactics World Impact: NPCs submit but plot against you Corruption Risk: Extreme - power comes at great cost The Curse System: Power Level: Stronger abilities unlock as corruption increases Side Effects: Physical and mental changes affect interactions Point of No Return: High corruption can lock you into the Domination path Redemption Mechanics: Difficult but possible to reverse corruption through sacrifice 6. 🏚️ Consequences & Rest Cycles The world reacts dynamically to your choices: World State Changes: Faction Power Shifts: Your actions strengthen or weaken various groups Regional Stability: Areas can become safer or more dangerous based on your involvement NPC Fates: Characters you've met continue their own stories, affected by your choices Rest and Reflection: Camp Conversations: Deep discussions with companions about recent events Moral Reflection: Kaelen contemplates his actions and their implications Planning Phase: Choose which contracts to pursue and which regions to explore next Long-term Consequences: Ending Variations: Multiple endings based on accumulated choices and final alignment Companion Fates: Each ally's ultimate destiny depends on your relationship and shared experiences World Legacy: The state of Velsingrad after your journey reflects your moral choices 🎯 Design Philosophy Player Agency Every choice matters, from major story decisions to small interactions with NPCs. The game respects player decisions and builds consequences organically. Meaningful Difficulty Challenges come not just from combat difficulty, but from moral complexity. The "right" choice isn't always clear, and powerful options come with significant costs. Emergent Storytelling While there's a central narrative, much of the story emerges from the intersection of player choices, companion reactions, and world state changes. Tactical Depth Combat should feel like solving puzzles rather than button mashing. Every encounter should offer multiple viable approaches. 🔄 Example Gameplay Session Here's how a typical gameplay session might unfold: Morning in Camp: Wake up to find a companion struggling with nightmares—do you comfort them or focus on practical concerns? Travel to Town: En route, encounter refugees fleeing bandits—help them, ignore them, or demand payment for protection? Challenge Board: Arrive at town to find three contracts available: Hunt down a supposed witch (high pay, moral uncertainty) Rescue miners trapped by a cave-in (time pressure, moderate reward) Investigate strange lights in the forest (mystery, unknown rewards) Preparation: Purchase supplies, upgrade equipment, have conversations with townspeople that reveal local politics and concerns. Mission Execution: Complete your chosen contract, making tactical and moral decisions that affect the outcome. Consequences: Return to find the town changed by your actions—perhaps the witch you killed was actually healing plague victims, or the miners you saved provide valuable intelligence. Evening Reflection: Back at camp, companions react to your choices, loyalty shifts, and new conversation options open up. Planning: Based on new information and changing circumstances, decide where to go next and what kind of leader Kaelen is becoming. This cycle creates a rich, interconnected experience where every choice ripples outward, creating a unique story for each player while maintaining the core themes of power, responsibility, and redemption. 🚀 Next Steps Ready to dive deeper? Explore these related systems: Combat System - Detailed tactical mechanics Magic System - How power and corruption intertwine Lore & Story - Learn more about the lore

docs/game-design/magic-system.html.title: ✨ Magic System | Ashes of Velsingrad Documentation
docs/game-design/magic-system.html.summary: ✨ Magic System The magic system in Ashes of Velsingrad is designed to be both a source of power and a dangerous temptation. It blends classical elemental magic with the dark and corrupting forces of the Curse, forcing players to carefully weigh their choices. 🌌 Core Principles of Magic Magic as a Double-Edged Sword Magic in Velsingrad is not just a tool; it is a force tied to the Curse, a malevolent energy that corrupts the land and its people. Every spell cast by Kaelen and certain companions may come at the cost of corruption, sanity, or loyalty. Schools of Magic There are six primary schools of magic, each with its own strengths, weaknesses, and thematic style: Fire – Destruction, area-of-effect damage, and burning status. Water – Healing, freezing, and battlefield control. Earth – Defense, terrain manipulation, and crowd control. Air – Lightning, mobility, and precision. Light – Protection, cleansing curses, and buffs. Darkness – Debuffs, fear, lifesteal, and summoning. 🔮 Spellcasting Mechanics Mana and Stamina Mana (MP): Consumed by most magical spells. Stamina (SP): Required for hybrid abilities that mix physical and magical effects. Corruption Points (CP): Accumulate when using dark or cursed spells. At high CP, Kaelen risks triggering Corruption Surges (uncontrollable magical outbursts). Spell Levels Each school of magic is divided into three tiers: Apprentice – Basic spells (e.g., Firebolt, Minor Heal). Adept – Intermediate control spells (e.g., Fire Wall, Ice Prison). Master – Devastating ultimate abilities (e.g., Meteor Storm, Blackened Sun). Casting Phases Casting a spell requires: Targeting Phase: Selecting a target or area. Channeling Phase: Some spells require multiple turns to charge. Execution Phase: The spell is unleashed, with potential side effects if interrupted. 🕯️ Corruption System Corruption Thresholds Using Darkness magic or invoking the Curse increases Kaelen’s corruption level: 0-25%: No penalties. 26-50%: Light magic effectiveness decreases by 10%. 51-75%: Random dark magic surges (unintended effects). 76-100%: Kaelen risks Losing Control, triggering a "Feral Corruption State" where he attacks allies and enemies indiscriminately. Cleansing Corruption Light Magic: Certain spells cleanse corruption. Sanctuaries: Special locations can purify the soul at a narrative cost. Sacrifices: Offering life energy (HP) or morale to lower corruption. 🧩 Elemental Interactions Magic can interact dynamically with the environment and other spells: Interaction Effect Fire + Oil Ignites the area, dealing extra burning damage. Water + Lightning Electrifies water, damaging all units standing in it. Ice + Fire Melts ice terrain, creating mud that slows movement. Earth + Air Creates dust storms, reducing visibility. 📜 Example Spells Fire School Firebolt (Apprentice): Single-target fire damage. Flame Surge (Adept): Creates a wall of fire that burns for 3 turns. Meteor Storm (Master): Calls down meteors over a wide area. Water School Minor Heal (Apprentice): Restores a small amount of HP. Ice Prison (Adept): Freezes enemies for 1 turn. Tidal Wave (Master): Pushes enemies back and deals water damage. Darkness School Drain Life (Apprentice): Steals HP from a target. Fear Wave (Adept): Reduces enemy morale, causing panic. Blackened Sun (Master): Creates an eclipse, dealing AoE curse damage and reducing accuracy. 🧙 Character Magic Specializations Kaelen Voss Kaelen's magic is heavily influenced by the Curse: Can access all schools, but dark magic grows stronger as corruption rises. At high corruption, unique "forbidden" spells unlock. Companion Mages Each mage companion specializes in one or two schools: Lyra, the Frost-Seer: Water + Air control. Elandor, the Ashbinder: Fire + Darkness. Seraphine, the Lightbearer: Light + Earth healing/support. 🎭 Role of Magic in the Narrative Magic is deeply tied to the storytelling and moral choices: Excessive use of dark magic affects companions’ loyalty (some may leave or betray Kaelen). Entire factions react to how much Kaelen relies on the Curse (e.g., Inquisition hunts him more aggressively). Special narrative events trigger when mastering certain schools, unlocking unique endings. 🏆 Advanced Magic Strategies Elemental Chains: Combine spells across schools for devastating effects (e.g., freeze enemies with water, then shatter them with earth). Environmental Control: Alter terrain to gain tactical advantages. Sacrificial Power: Channel corruption to cast ultimate spells earlier, but risk losing control. "Magic is not light or dark by nature—it is the hand that wields it, and the heart that shapes it." — Seraphine, the Lightbearer
